title: Oobleck wraps to cross a glass-covered kitchen
opening_scene: Tyler stands barefoot at the kitchen doorway. The floor is a carpet of shattered glass from an exploded cabinet. On the counter to his left: a box of cornstarch, a water faucet, a folded cotton dishtowel. Smoke alarm screams in the background — something burning on the stove.
goal: Cross the glass-covered floor barefoot without cutting feet.
constraints: Floor fully covered in glass shards
No shoes anywhere in scene
Sliding chair would crush glass into your hand on climbing
Time pressure (smoke alarm)

default_solutions_eliminated.why_eliminated: Body weight concentrates on shards; glass pushes between cloth fibers
Cuts hands; can't carry items; exhausting
Chair legs grind glass into 4 cutting points
Powder doesn't change cutting mechanics

principle_solution: Mix cornstarch + water at 2:1 by volume → oobleck. Soak dishtowel in it. Wrap both feet. Step. The shear-thickening polymer is soft when bending the foot but locks rigid under high-rate puncture pressure.
hook_line: If you were standing in your kitchen and the cabinet just shattered glass across the floor — you have no shoes, your phone is across the room, and the only things on the counter are cornstarch, water, and a dishtowel — how do you cross? Just like I tested with my Indestructible Shoes — the shear-thickening non-Newtonian fluid stays soft when you bend it slowly but locks rigid the instant something tries to puncture it.
vfx_notes: Real glass on real floor; safety mat erased via clean plate; mannequin or body cam for cutaways.

hook_line: If you arrived at a paintball arena and your gear bag had nothing but 50 newspapers, duct tape, and scissors — and the opponents are loading their guns now — how do you stop those paintballs from welting through to your skin? Just like I built my Bulletproof Batman Armour — composite material layering distributes impact across the panel.
